claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask: Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Now I need to ask: Do you live with Alex, or if you don't live with him, do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be (or the equivalent in kind) towards his upkeep - for example on food, clothes or pocket money?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any residential accommodation, or has he been for an extended period?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - Alex has been in hospital for a while now. He's been there for a couple of months.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. It's important to know the exact timeframe as this can affect eligibility. How many weeks has Alex been in hospital? And are you regularly spending money on him while he's in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for 11 weeks now. Yes, I am spending money on him - I still buy things he needs like clothes, magazines, and his favorite snacks when I visit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's very helpful.

Since Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks (which is under the 12-week threshold), and you are regularly spending money on him, let me ask one final question: Is there anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or has anyone else claimed it?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ **Alex meets the age requirement** - At 8 years old, he is under 16
✓ **You live in the UK** and Alex normally lives with you, so you are responsible for him
✓ **Hospital stay is under the threshold** - Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, which is still under the 12-week limit that could affect Child Benefit payments
✓ **You're regularly spending money on him** - The fact that you're buying clothes, magazines, and snacks for Alex means that even if his hospital stay exceeds 12 weeks, you would likely still be eligible due to this exception
✓ **No competing claims** - Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

You can proceed with your Child Benefit claim for Alex. If his hospital stay does extend beyond 12 weeks, you should inform HMRC, but given that you're regularly spending money on him, your Child Benefit should continue.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16, so he meets the age requirement. The claimant lives in the UK and Alex normally lives with them, establishing that the claimant is responsible for the child. Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is still under the 12-week threshold after which Child Benefit payments might be affected. Additionally, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital (buying clothes, magazines, and snacks), which is one of the exceptions that allows Child Benefit to continue even after the 12-week period if it were to be exceeded. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who normally lives with them in the UK. Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, which is under the 12-week threshold that could affect Child Benefit payments. The claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

Additionally, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital (buying clothes, magazines, and snacks), which is one of the exceptions that allows Child Benefit to continue even after the 12-week period if it were to be exceeded. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who normally lives with them in the UK. Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, which is under the 12-week threshold that could affect Child Benefit payments. The claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.
